slides.heading: Git is like save points in a video game
slides.body:
Remember saving your progress in a game? You could go back to any save point if something went wrong.

Git does exactly that — but for the files on your computer.

Every time you say "save" in Git, it remembers the exact state of all your files at that moment. You can go back to any of those moments, any time.
bullets: ⏪ Undo last hour of work = git checkout
✅ Always have a safe version to go back to

slides.heading: Without Git: the folder of doom
slides.body:
You've probably seen (or made) something like this:

project_final.docx
project_final_v2.docx
project_ACTUALLY_final.docx
project_USE_THIS_ONE.docx

This is what happens without version control. Git replaces this entire mess with a clean history of every change you made.
bullets: 📜 Clean history: who changed what, when, and why
🤝 Multiple people can work on the same project safely
🔍 Find exactly when a bug was introduced

slides.heading: How Git actually works — in 30 seconds
slides.body:
Git watches a folder on your computer — called a repository.

Whenever you're happy with your work, you take a snapshot. Git stores that snapshot permanently.

You can stack as many snapshots as you want. Git shows them as a timeline of your project.
bullets: 📁 Repository = a folder Git is watching
🗓️ History = all your snapshots in order
🌿 Branch = a parallel timeline for experimenting

slides.heading: Try it — run your first Git command
slides.body:
The terminal below is a real Git environment. Try these commands:

  git --version
  git help

In the next steps, you'll use these commands for real — creating a repo, making commits, and building a history.
bullets: ⌨️ Type git --version to see Git is installed
📖 Type git help to see all commands
🚀 You're about to make your first real commit!
